articles.title: Manx Wildlife Week: Connecting People with Nature on the Isle of Man
articles.summary: The Isle of Man is celebrating its natural environment with Manx Wildlife Week, a series of over 25 events organized by Manx National Heritage (MNH). The week includes beach cleans, bird watching, countryside walks, workshops, lectures, and macro-photography. First held in 2018, the event originated from the island's involvement with an online wildlife atlas. Organizer Laura McCoy, MNH's curator of natural history, said the week aims to celebrate the work of local environmental groups and encourage people to connect with nature. The events are free and open to all ages, appealing to both casual nature lovers and those interested in scientific activities. Examples of events include a bee trail with Friends of the Earth, a bird watching session for young birders, and a wildlife obstacle course with Manx Wildlife Trust. The week has gained momentum, with more groups partnering each year.

articles.title: Giant Octopus Ruled Cretaceous Seas
articles.summary: For decades, paleontologists believed that the top predators in Late Cretaceous oceans were large vertebrates like mosasaurs, plesiosaurs, and sharks. Invertebrates were considered prey. However, a new study published in Science challenges this view. Researchers have discovered fossilized jaws of a giant octopus, Nanaimoteuthis haggarti, which may have reached lengths of up to 19 meters. Octopuses rarely fossilize because their bodies are soft, but their chitinous beaks can be preserved. The team used a technique called Digital Fossil Mining, which involves grinding rock in microscopic layers and using AI to detect fossils. The beaks showed wear and scratches, indicating active predation. Size estimates were made using allometric calculations based on modern octopuses. This discovery suggests that invertebrates could also be apex predators, reshaping our understanding of ancient food webs.

articles.title: The Search for a Fifth Force in Our Solar System
articles.summary: A fundamental challenge in physics is that the universe behaves differently at different scales. Observations of distant galaxies show that the universe is expanding in a way that cannot be fully explained by Einstein's theory of general relativity. This has led to the concepts of dark energy and dark matter. However, within our solar system, all measurements align perfectly with standard physics. This mismatch is known as the 'Great Disconnect.' A new study by NASA physicist Slava Turyshev proposes that a 'fifth force' might be hiding in our solar system, but its effects are masked by a phenomenon called screening. In dense environments like near the Sun, this force becomes very weak, making it difficult to detect. Two models explain this: the chameleon model, where the force's strength changes with density, and the Vainshtein screening model, where gravity suppresses the force. Turyshev suggests that extremely precise experiments, possibly using new space missions, could reveal this force. If found, it could help explain dark energy and dark matter, revolutionizing our understanding of the universe.

articles.title: Astronomers Discover Puzzling Cosmic Explosion: A Possible Superkilonova
articles.summary: When massive stars die, they explode as supernovae, scattering elements across space. A rarer event, a kilonova, occurs when two neutron stars merge, producing heavy elements like gold. Only one kilonova, GW170817, has been confirmed. Now, astronomers may have found a second candidate, AT2025ulz, but it behaves strangely. Detected on August 18, 2025, by LIGO and Virgo, the gravitational-wave signal suggested a merger involving at least one unusually small object. Hours later, the Zwicky Transient Facility found a fading red source in the same sky region. Initially resembling the 2017 kilonova, AT2025ulz brightened again, turned blue, and showed hydrogen – typical of a supernova. This puzzled scientists because supernovae usually don't produce detectable gravitational waves. Led by Mansi Kasliwal of Caltech, the team proposes that this could be a "superkilonova" – a kilonova triggered by a supernova. If confirmed, it would be the first such observation, offering new insights into stellar evolution and element formation. The study was published in The Astrophysical Journal Letters.

articles.title: Land, Not Ocean, Is the Main Source of Airborne Microplastics
articles.summary: Microplastics are tiny plastic particles that have been found in the atmosphere worldwide, even in remote areas. They can be inhaled by humans and animals, raising health concerns. A new study from the University of Vienna, published in Nature, provides a clearer picture of where these airborne microplastics originate. The researchers compiled 2,782 measurements from global studies and used computer models to estimate emissions from land and ocean sources. Their key finding is that land emits over 20 times more microplastic particles than the ocean. However, the mass of microplastics from the ocean is larger because ocean particles are bigger. Earlier models had overestimated emissions, but after recalibrating with real-world data, land still dominated. The study highlights the need for more measurements to reduce uncertainties, especially regarding sources like traffic and particle size distribution.

articles.title: Ancient Antarctic Ice Reveals Climate Puzzle
articles.summary: Scientists studying ancient ice from Antarctica have uncovered a surprising pattern in Earth's climate over the past 3 million years. Two new studies in the journal Nature show that while the planet gradually cooled, levels of greenhouse gases in the atmosphere declined only slightly. This finding challenges previous assumptions about the relationship between greenhouse gases and global temperature. The research comes from the Center for Oldest Ice Exploration (COLDEX), led by Oregon State University. The team analyzed ice from Allan Hills, Antarctica, which contains distorted layers that provide snapshots of past climate. One study used noble gases trapped in air bubbles to estimate ocean temperature changes. The results show that average ocean temperatures dropped by about 2 to 2.5 degrees Celsius over 3 million years. Most of the cooling occurred early, between 3 million and 2 million years ago, coinciding with the formation of large ice sheets in the Northern Hemisphere. Surface ocean temperatures cooled more gradually until about 1 million years ago. The studies help fill gaps in our understanding of Earth's climate history and raise new questions about how heat moves between the ocean surface and deep layers.
